cards.front: In Macbeth, what spiritual and psychological disturbances does Macbeth report in the Act 2 chamber aftermath after Duncan’s killing?
cards.back: Macbeth cannot say “Amen” and hears a voice cry that he has murdered sleep.
cards.explanation: Macbeth says that “Amen” sticks in his throat despite his need for blessing. He then reports hearing a voice accuse him of murdering innocent sleep.
cards.memoryTip: Retrieve the disturbance in two stages: blocked blessing → accusing voice about sleep.

choices.text: Macbeth cannot say “Amen” and hears a voice cry that he has murdered sleep.
choices.feedback: Correct: Macbeth says “Amen” stuck in his throat and reports the cry “Macbeth does murder sleep.”

choices.text: Macbeth says “Amen” and hears a voice promise that he will sleep.
choices.feedback: This reverses both reports: Macbeth says that “Amen” stuck in his throat and that the voice cried “Sleep no more!”
choices.misconception: Reversing the blocked prayer and the accusing voice.

choices.text: Lady Macbeth cannot say “Amen” and hears the voice cry that she has murdered sleep.
choices.feedback: This transfers both experiences from Macbeth to Lady Macbeth; Macbeth describes the blocked “Amen” and reports the voice.
choices.misconception: Swapping Macbeth’s reported experience with Lady Macbeth’s.

choices.text: Macbeth cannot say “Amen” but hears a voice say that Lady Macbeth has murdered sleep.
choices.feedback: The blocked “Amen” belongs to Macbeth, but the voice also names Macbeth—not Lady Macbeth—as the one who has murdered sleep.
choices.misconception: Keeping the prayer detail while transferring the accusation to Lady Macbeth.

cards.sourceExcerpt: I could not say “Amen,” When they did say, “God bless us.” LADY MACBETH. Consider it not so deeply. MACBETH. But wherefore could not I pronounce “Amen”? I had most need of blessing, and “Amen” Stuck in my throat. LADY MACBETH. These deeds must not be thought After these ways; so, it will make us mad. MACBETH. Methought I heard a voice cry, “Sleep no more! Macbeth does murder sleep,”
